output_text:
Flagged a fatal contradiction between the reported numbers and the claims, trimmed heavy redundancy about "role specialization," and produced a revised version that honestly reflects what the data show.

## The blocking problem

**Your numbers contradict your conclusion.** The baseline posts FPR 7.2% / FNR 11.8%. BioCrew posts FPR 9.6% / FNR 13.4%. BioCrew is worse on both axes. Yet the text claims BioCrew "substantially reduces both error modes," calls 9.6% "a meaningful improvement over the baseline's 7.2%," and credits role specialization as the reason. No amount of prose polishing fixes this — a reviewer will catch it in the first pass and the paper is dead.

Before any rewrite is meaningful, you have to decide which is wrong: the numbers, the direction of the comparison (did the columns get swapped?), or the conclusion. The revision below assumes the numbers are correct and the conclusion was wrong. If instead the numbers are swapped, swap them and the revision still mostly works with sign flips.

## Other problems

1. **Redundancy.** "Role specialization" is reasserted 5+ times in ~350 words ("specialized agents," "distinct functional role," "clearly delimited role," "role-specialized architecture," "role-specialized design," "specific functional duty," "distinct roles for distinct subtasks," "different agents handle different responsibilities"). Say it once, well.
2. **No statistics.** With n=412, the FPR difference of 2.4 points has a confidence interval you can compute; report it. Without CIs or a McNemar/bootstrap test, "substantially" is unsupported either way.
3. **Baseline is underspecified.** "Same instructions concatenated into one context window" — same model, temperature, tool access, retries? If BioCrew calls tools and the baseline doesn't, the comparison isn't about role specialization at all.
4. **No ablation.** You attribute the effect to role specialization but never isolate it (e.g., single-agent-with-tools, or BioCrew-without-Critic). The causal claim is unearned.
5. **Forward reference to Section 2** is asserted, not argued.
6. **"Multi-agent decomposition is the more reliable design choice"** is a population-level claim from a single benchmark.

## Revised text (assuming the numbers as written)

> We introduce BioCrew, a multi-agent framework that assembles variant-annotation pipelines from natural-language clinical queries. Three role-specialized LLM agents collaborate: a Planner decomposes the query, a Tooler selects bioinformatics utilities (bcftools, VEP, ANNOVAR), and a Critic reviews intermediate outputs. The design hypothesis is that dividing planning, tool selection, and verification across agents reduces error compared to a single generalist agent issuing the full pipeline in one pass.
>
> We test this on ClinVar-Bench, 412 clinical variant queries paired with gold-standard annotations. The baseline is a single GPT-4 agent given the concatenated Planner/Tooler/Critic instructions in one context window, with identical tool access and decoding parameters. BioCrew records a false-positive rate of 9.6% (95% CI: X–Y) and a false-negative rate of 13.4% (X–Y); the baseline records 7.2% (X–Y) and 11.8% (X–Y). On this benchmark, role specialization as instantiated in BioCrew does **not** improve over the single-agent baseline and trends modestly worse on both error modes; the per-query paired difference is [significant / not significant] under McNemar's test (p = Z).
>
> Two readings are consistent with these numbers. First, the decomposition may introduce inter-agent communication errors — e.g., the Tooler acting on a Planner summary that omits constraints present in the original query — that exceed the gains from specialization. Second, single-pass GPT-4 may already internalize the Planner/Tooler/Critic decomposition implicitly, leaving little headroom for an explicit agentic scaffold. The ablations in Section X (BioCrew without Critic; single agent with tool access) localize the effect to [—]. We return in the Discussion to when multi-agent decomposition is and is not warranted for clinical annotation.

The third paragraph of the original should be cut entirely; it adds no information beyond restating paragraph one.

## Uncertainty

I have not verified the ClinVar-Bench dataset, the GPT-4 baseline configuration, or whether the FPR/FNR values were transcribed correctly — the contradiction may be a typo rather than a real result, and only you can confirm. CI and McNemar values are placeholders. The two mechanistic readings in the revised paragraph are plausible hypotheses, not findings; keep them only if your ablations actually support one.
